coredata.dc:title: Tunable Emission of the laser reduced Graphene Quantum Dots be used as Wavelength Shifter layers in Silicon-based UV Detectors
coredata.dc:creator: Azimirad, R.
Najafi, V.
Safa, S.
coredata.dc:description: A novel and effectual method is presented to tune the fluorescence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by reducing their surface using laser irradiation. The laser exposure time of the GQDs suspension was considered as the main parameter. According to PL spectra, the wavelength emission of as-prepared GQDs blue-shifted by increasing the laser irradiation time due to a decrease in the degree of surface oxidation. Interestingly, the FTIR result confirmed that the oxygen-containing surface functional groups had also been reduced. Subsequently, our GQDs with different emission color was embedded in a polymer matrix and deposited on a silicon photodetector as wavelength down-shifting layers. The results show that the photocurrent of the photodetector with shifter film under UV light increased more than 3 fold compared with the bare detector. In addition, the response of detectors has increased more than 10 times.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs
Laser treatment
Photodetector
Photocurrent

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQDs films for electrical characterization
UV Sensing with wavelength shifter layer

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is based upon research funded by Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) under project No. 99028772.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
a) The images of GQDs suspension after laser therapy under excitation of a 370 nm UV lamp and b) their PL spectra (
λ
e x
= 350 nm).

a) Uv–Vis absorbance of GQDs suspension for different times of laser exposure and b) Raman spectra (
λ
e x
= 532 nm) of as prepared GQDs and after 30 min of laser irradiation.
a) XPS survey spectra and well-fitted C1s XPS spectra of b) as prepared GQDs and c) after 30 min of laser irradiation. d) FTIR spectra of GQDs for different times of laser exposure.
TEM image and size distribution histogram (inside) of a) as-prepared GQDs and b) after 30 min of laser irradiation.
The current–voltage plot of deposited GQDs after various times (0–30 min) of laser irradiation on the silicon wafer.
a) Photocurrent of the bare silicon photodiode under visible and UV light and b) selective response for bare Si photodiode.

The time-dependent photocurrent for all detectors with wavelength shifter layers on Si photodiode under UV-light irradiation for a) low GQDs concentration (2 mL) layer, b) medium GQDs concentration (4 mL) layer and c) high GQDs concentration (8 mL) layer. d) Selective response of the UV-sensitizer photodiode with a wavelength shifter layer including laser-reduced GQDs for the 20 min duration and medium concentration (4 mL).
a) Sensitivity and b) responsivity of the UV-sensitizer photodiode with a wavelength shifter layer (medium concentration) and bare photodiode.
Sensing parameters of all prepared detectors and other works for comparison.

head.ce:title: Tunable Emission of the laser reduced Graphene Quantum Dots be used as Wavelength Shifter layers in Silicon-based UV Detectors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Malek-Ashtar University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Malek-Ashtar University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Physics, Semnan University, Semnan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Physics, Semnan University, Semnan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, University of Zabol, Zabol, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, University of Zabol, Zabol, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel and effectual method is presented to tune the fluorescence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) by reducing their surface using laser irradiation. The laser exposure time of the GQDs suspension was considered as the main parameter. According to PL spectra, the wavelength emission of as-prepared GQDs blue-shifted by increasing the laser irradiation time due to a decrease in the degree of surface oxidation. Interestingly, the FTIR result confirmed that the oxygen-containing surface functional groups had also been reduced. Subsequently, our GQDs with different emission color was embedded in a polymer matrix and deposited on a silicon photodetector as wavelength down-shifting layers. The results show that the photocurrent of the photodetector with shifter film under UV light increased more than 3 fold compared with the bare detector. In addition, the response of detectors has increased more than 10 times.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are promising fluorescent zero-dimensional carbon nanostructures that have unique optical and electronic properties [1–3]. It is well known that they exhibit exceptional tunable excitation emission properties. So far, researchers have succeeded in synthesizing GQDs with different emission wavelengths from ultraviolet (UV) to red [4,5]. This feature alongside Graphene's inherent nontoxicity and stability leads to GQDs having received a lot of attention as promising materials for use in a wide range of optoelectronic applications [6,7]. In particular, GQDs have developed novel ultrafast and high-gain response photo-detection devices and created high-sensitive photodetectors [8]. For instance, improving sensitivity using GQDs in UV photodetectors has received considerable research due to their importance in various fields such as environmental monitoring, missile warning systems, health gadgets, space research, flame detection, etc. [9]. GQDs can be used as a sensitizer in Metal-Semiconductor-Metal (MSM) UV photodetectors or as semiconductor in p–n heterojunction type [10,11]. MSM-type UV detectors are commonly announced as reliable UV sensors, but they have some drawbacks such as low response time, strong persistent photoconductivity and dependency on semiconductor surface defects [12,13]. Recently, GQDs have been used on silicon photodetectors as a down-shifting layer in order to wavelength matching between UV light and the silicon band gap. These types of sensors have shown very low response time and appropriate efficiency [14,15]. However, tuning the color-emitting of GQDs under UV excitation has always been a challenge. The emission properties of GQDs are significantly dependent on several factors such as size, shape, edge structure, defects, and surface functional groups [16,17]. In general, the photoluminescence behavior of GQDs is influenced by the relationship between the carbon core and the functional groups on the surface and edges. It is well known that the higher degree of surface oxidation can lead to more surface defects and a red shift in emission wavelength [18]. Therefore, surface passivation and modification of GQDs is considered as an effective method for adjusting emission wavelength, increasing quantum yield and decreasing the decay time [19].

To date, various methods have been introduced for surface modification of GQDs. Some of the main facilitator ways are treatment by sodium tetrahydroborate [20] and hydrazine hydrate [21], photoelectrochemical method [22] and microwave assistant irradiation [23]. Herein, we proposed laser irradiation at different times to modify the surface of the GQDs and tune their luminescence under UV excitation. Laser radiation is a low-cost and environmentally friendly process to surface reduction of GQDs, as it does not produce any harmful byproducts or waste materials. Furthermore, it is a rapid, efficient, precise and non-contact technique which means reduces the risk of damage to the GQDs and ensures a clean modification process. After reducing the surface of as-prepared GQDs, they were embedded in a polymer matrix and deposited on a silicon photodetector as a luminescence down-shifting layer. UV photodetectors fabricated using wavelength-shifting layers on a silicon photodiode have several advantages. They are sensitive to a wide range of UV wavelengths, have high quantum efficiency and are cost-effective. Additionally, they can be easily integrated into electronic systems, making them suitable for various applications.

GQDs were synthesized through an electrochemical exfoliation method according to our previous works [24,25]. Briefly, two graphitic electrodes were thoroughly cleaned and placed in an oxidizing solution containing 49 mL ethanol, 1 mL water and 380 mg sodium hydroxide (NaOH) as the exfoliator agent. The electrodes were held at 25 V for 40 min, then the darkened solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 4 h. Finally, the as-prepared solution was separated by column chromatography using a silica gel column with a length of 300 mm and a volume of 50 mL.

The surface modification of GQDs was carried out using a 532 nm Nd:YAG pulsed laser with pulse duration of 5 ns, a repetition rate of 10 Hz and 0.3 W power. The laser provided pulse energy up to 300 mJ in a beam diameter of 7 mm. In a quartz sample vial, 3 mL dimethylformamide (DMF) was added to 12 mL of the obtained GQDs solution and exposed to 15 mJ laser irradiation for 0, 15, 20, 25, and 30 min. Afterward, column chromatography was employed once again to purify highly fluorescent GQDs from other portions. To simplify further discussion, the GQDs are labeled 0 to 30 min related to exposing time, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs films for electrical characterization
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the electrical properties, GQDs were deposited directly on the silicon wafer using the electrophoretic deposition (EPD) method and Na metal ions as charging agents. The electrolyte consisted of a solution of GQDs (0 to 30 min) combined with 0.1 M NaOH solution which was sonicated for 10 min. Graphite was used as the positive electrode and n-type silicon wafer as the negative electrode (dimensions: 1 × 1 cm) at a distance of 3 cm from each other. The deposition was carried out by applying the working potential of 10 V to electrodes for 1 h.

A commercial UV-curing adhesive (Loxeal-30/20) containing various concentrations of GQDs were used as a down-shifting layer. For this purpose, 2, 4 and 8 mL from the final GQDs solutions are perfectly mixed with 5 g adhesive and deposited on top of the photodetector (Silicon PIN photodiodes, a four array of TEMD5010 × 01, Vishay Semiconductors, 5 × 4.24 × 1.12 mm3 with 4 × 7.5 mm2 sensitive surface) by the doctor-blade technique.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the samples were plotted to study the surface functional groups by using a Shimadzu-8400S spectrometer in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. Photoluminescence (PL) properties at room temperature were plotted by employing a photoluminescence spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer LS55) at the 350 nm excitation wavelength. The optical characteristics of the samples were analyzed by ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Lambda 35) within the range of 190–800 nm. Raman spectra were acquired using a Thermo Nicolet Almega XR model at the excitation wavelength of 532 nm. The current–voltage (I–V) characteristics were recorded by sweeping the bias voltage. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to study the chemical state variations by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB250xi). Finally, the UV photocurrent of samples was measured under a 12 W Philips black UV-light lamp with illumination in the range of 350–400 nm.

Fig. 1 a shows a luminescent photograph of GQDs under excitation of a 370 nm UV lamp. As can be seen, GQDs exhibit bright emissions with tunable color related to laser exposure time. As prepared quantum dots emitted green luminescence while blue shift occurs in emission wavelength after laser treatment. The PL spectra of GQDs have been shown in Fig. 1b. PL spectrum of unmodified GQDs (indicated in the Fig. 1b as 0 min.) which were excited at 350 nm have a characteristic peak at 502 nm. By increasing the time of laser exposure, the peak is broadened and shifted to shorter wavelengths. After 30 min of laser irradiation, the peak has located at 416 nm and becomes sharper. This behavior can be due to the reduction of surface functional groups caused by the interaction with the laser beam. Similar to other graphene structures, the presence of oxygenated functional groups such as OH, COOH, C = O, etc. on GQDs creates surface states between main π-π* energy levels originated from C = C aromatic bonds. Functional groups create different energy levels that act as the trap states and cause photoluminescence when excited at a certain wavelength. By increasing the level of surface oxidation which causes more surface defects, the wavelength of photoluminescence emission shifts to higher wavelengths [26,27].

Furthermore, it is clearly seen from Fig. 1b after surface passivation, the PL intensity is enhanced. Increasing emission peak intensity can be attributed to the increasing PL quantum yield due to improving recombination of electron-hole pairs in local state of sp2 carbon [28]. Sun et al. [20] reported a photo−reducing method to prepare bright blue luminescent GQDs from green fluorescent GQDs by UV irradiation as the reducing agent. Their results show the quantum yield of photo−reduced GQDs was increased 3.7 times. The UV–Vis absorbance spectra of the GQDs suspension for different times of laser exposure are shown in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2, the UV region peak increased and redshifted with the increased laser irradiation time. This absorption peak is related to the π-π* transition in the aromatic bands and its increment means restoration of the electronic conjugation after laser treatment [29,30]. It should also be mentioned that, compared with as-prepared GQDs, other laser-reduced GQDs demonstrate a shoulder at about 260 nm which we speculated was caused due to surface change of GQDs.

The Raman scattering spectra of as-prepared and laser-reduced GQDs are shown in Fig. 2b. The two main bands in the carbon materials spectrum are known as the D-band at ∼ 1350 cm−1 and the G-band band at ∼ 1580 cm−1. However, both spectra show typical graphitic features with ID/IG = 0.54 for as-prepared GQDs and ID/IG = 0.69 for laser-reduced GQDs. As is clear, after laser exposure the D-mode became more intense while the intensity of the G-band decreased. Generally, D-band is related to symmetry breaking at defects and edges and G-band occurs because of C-C deformations in a plane. So it can be concluded, due to laser-induced bond breaking, the D-mode intensity increases as a result of sp2 network damage [31]. Moreover, a small shift towards higher frequencies is observed for both Raman peaks of laser-reduced GQDs compared to the D- and G-bands of as-prepared GQDs which can be due to the size reduction of GQDs. Dervishi et al [32] have noted both D and G-band frequencies in GQDs Raman spectra are quite size-dependent and decrease by increasing GQDs size.

Changes in the surface of the as-prepared and laser-reduced GQDs have been detected by XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the XPS spectra of both as-prepared GQDs and after 30 min laser irradiation indicate three main peaks at 284.6 eV, 400 eV and 532 eV associated with C1s, N1s and O1s core-level respectively. After the reduction of the GQDs surface by laser irradiation, the O/C atomic ratio was decreased to 38 %, while for the as-prepared GQDs, it was 73 % which shows that the surface deoxygenation process has been successful. Furthermore, the XPS peak deconvolution of C1s core-level of the laser-reduced GQDs, as compared to the peaks of the as-prepared ones are presented in Fig. 3b and c. Each C1s XPS spectrum can be separated into five distinct peaks corresponding to C–C bonds at 284.6 eV, C–N bonds at 286 eV, C–O bonds at 286.8 eV, C = O and O–C = O at 288 and 289 eV respectively. From the comparison of Fig.3b and c, it is clear that after surface passivation of GQDs by pulsed laser irradiation, the peaks associated with oxygen-containing functional groups were reduced. According to the analysis results, the C–O and C = O percentage drops from 15.82 % to 6.56 % and from 5.42 % to 4.11 % respectively after treatment which confirms the above-mentioned conclusion. To verify the XPS data on the reduction of functional groups on the GQD surface by laser irradiation, FTIR spectroscopy was used (Fig. 3d). The main peaks related to absorption bands for the as-prepared GQDs were observed at 3438 cm−1 (O–H stretching band), 3189 cm−1 and 2967 cm−1 (C–H stretching band), 2406 cm−1 (N-related band), 2071 cm−1 (C-H bending), 1628 cm−1 (C = O stretching band), 1404 cm−1 (COOH stretching band) and 1028 cm−1 (C-O stretching vibrations). After laser irradiation, a gradual decrease is observed in the intensity of similar peaks in laser-reduced GQDs FTIR spectrum due to eliminating oxygen-containing functional groups from their surface. However, both XPS and FTIR data show that completely removing all surface oxygen of GQDs is impossible which may be related to the size and the edge effects of GQDs. It means that some oxygen-containing groups at the edges (such as carboxyl groups, –COOH) cannot be completely reduced, as shown by Zhu et al [20].

Fig 4. shows the transmission electron microscope (TEM) images and size distribution histogram of as-prepared GQDs and GQDs after 30 min of laser irradiation. On average, the size of more than 80 % of as-prepared GQDs is between 4 and 7 nm and their size distribution is completely symmetrical. On the other hand, the GQDs which have been exposed to laser radiation have relatively smaller sizes. The diameters of laser-reduced GQDs were mainly distributed in the range of 2–6 nm. This means that the laser beam changed the size of the carbon core in addition to surface reduction.

The effect of the surface reduction processes on the electronic property of the laser-reduced GQDs was examined using the current–voltage (I –V) characteristics of deposited GQDs films. According to Fig. 5 , the slope of the curves increased after surface passivation by laser irradiation, indicating an increase in the surface conductivity of GQDs. Although, re-oxidizing GQDs during the electrophoretic deposition has caused a lot of differences in surface resistance of GQDs not to be observed. Furthermore, deviation from linear behavior of current–voltage curves may be due to Joule heating effects from the bias current or nonideal ohmic contacts between the GQDs and the electrodes [33,34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV Sensing with wavelength shifter layer
ce:section.ce:para:
The silicon photosensor or photodiode is a semiconductor device used to detect and measure visible light. They are so popular due to their strong and quick response, high stability, and low cost. However, UV light cannot be detected selectively by photodiodes because the band gap with UV photon energy is inconsistent. Fig. 6 a shows the photocurrent of the photodiode exposing by visible light and UV light over regular 10-seconds switching cycles. As can be seen, the current caused by the radiation of UV photons is extremely low and difficult to measure. In addition, a suitable sensor should be able to detect UV in the presence of visible light. So, we introduce the Selective Response (SR) parameter as follows:
S e l e c t i v e
R e s p o n s e =
P h o t o c u r r e n
t
( V i s + U V )
- Ph o t o c u r r e n
t
( V i s )
Ph o t o c u r r e n
t
( V i s + U V )
× 100
According to the above relationship, it is determined that this parameter for the Si photodiode is too poor.

Fig. 7 depicts the photocurrent of the UV- sensitizer photodiode with a wavelength shifter layer including exposed GQDs at different times of laser irradiation. According to the Fig. 7, all the detectors with the shifter layer had a much higher photocurrent value. Between layers, wavelength shifter film which contains GQDs that have been exposed to the laser for 20 min, has the highest efficiency. Considering the GQDs PL spectrum, it was found that the broadest emission peak is associated with these GQDs. Moreover, the photocurrent of the detectors with a medium concentration of GQDs in the shifter layer is a larger amount than the other layers. The highest amount of photocurrent in these detectors is 175 µA which is more than 3 times higher compared to the photodiode without UV-sensitizer layer. The photocurrent of the detector is enhanced by the down-conversion luminescence of the shifter films. Down conversion luminescence is a process in which a material absorbs high-energy photons, such as UV light, and then emits lower-energy photons, such as visible or infrared light. In fact, according to the sensitivity of the silicon photodiode the generated photocurrent for wavelengths below 340 nm (UV range) is very low. The shifter films during the down-conversion luminescence process absorb UV light and emit light in the range of 420 to 520 nm. At these wavelengths, the photocurrent produced by the photodiode is much higher [35]. One of the most important advantages of UV detectors with wavelength shifter layers on Si photodiode is their quick response times. A fast response time is critical for applications where rapid detection of changes in UV radiation is necessary, such as in monitoring UV light sources or in process control applications. As seen in Fig. 7 (a-c), all detectors have a response time of less than 1.5 s, making them suitable for special applications such as flame detection. Sensing parameters for all prepared detectors are shown in Table 1 . In addition, details of other works in the literature are included in the table for comparison.

Fig. 7d shows the selective response for the photodiode with the wavelength shifter layer including laser-reduced GQDs for the 20 min duration and medium concentration (4 mL). Compared to the calculated selective response for the Si photodiode, this parameter has increased more than 10 times which can lead to the construction of high-performance UV-detectors.

The sensitivity of the detectors to detect the threshold response to UV light for the UV sensitizer and the bare photodiode is shown in Fig. 8 a. The results show that the sensors with the shifter layer have an acceptable photocurrent compared to the bare photodiode by reducing the received light power. Furthermore, Responsivity parameter of the all UV-sensitizer photodiode were calculated and presented in Fig. 8b.

Using an electrochemical technique and pulsed laser irradiations, we successfully synthesized varied fluorescence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to fabricate a UV-sensitized layer. Laser-exposed GQDs emitted bright emissions with tunable color and by increasing the time of laser exposure, their Photoluminescence peak became broadened and shifted to shorter wavelengths. Based on the FTIR and Raman scattering results, the oxygen-containing surface functional groups of GQDs had been reduced and quantum yield increased due to the decrease in the level of surface oxidation. Subsequently, a luminescent down-shifting film was created by embedding GQDs with different emission colors in a polymer matrix. The photocurrent of the UV-sensitizer photodiode with wavelength shifter layer was a noticeable increase which improved the response of the Si photodiode under UV-light irradiation.
R. Azimirad: . V. Najafi: . S. Safa: Conceptualization, Writing – Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is based upon research funded by Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) under project No. 99028772.
